prompt: Name a song you find genuinely moving — any language, any era — and describe, in a short paragraph, why it works on you.
response: Leonard Cohen’s “Hallelujah” (1984) moves me deeply—not because of its now-ubiquitous covers, but because of Cohen’s original, weathered voice and the song’s raw, sacred-yet-human tension. It weaves biblical imagery with intimate longing, turning spiritual doubt and earthly desire into a single, aching prayer. The genius lies in its contradictions: it’s reverent and irreverent, broken yet beautiful, confessing failure even as it reaches for transcendence. Every time I hear “It’s a cold and it’s a broken Hallelujah,” I feel both the ache of imperfection and the stubborn hope that beauty can still emerge from it.
